title: Europe Will Never Be an AI Superpower
gist: Global Public Policy Institute analysts Thorsten Benner and Jakob Hensing argue Europe cannot become an AI superpower and that the EuroStack full-independence strategy is both unrealistic and too weak for a near-term powerful-AI scenario; instead Europe should act as a middle power — leveraging ASML/Siemens Energy chokepoints, courting U.S. hyperscaler data-center investment, and doubling down on industrial AI — to secure continued access to frontier U.S. models.

insights.claim: The U.S. government's June 2026 decision to impose sweeping export controls on Anthropic's most advanced models, Mythos 5 and Fable 5, exposed Europe's dependence on U.S. frontier AI providers and showed such restrictions could recur at any time.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner, Jakob Hensing
insights.so_what: A concrete trigger event underscoring supply-chain/model-access risk for any Europe-exposed AI strategy or portfolio.
insights.quote: made Europe’s dependence on leading U.S. artificial intelligence providers starkly clear

insights.claim: Growing scarcity of AI computing power creates an ongoing risk that U.S. political intervention will prioritize U.S. users' compute needs over European ones, independent of any formal export controls.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner, Jakob Hensing
insights.so_what: Frames a chronic, not one-off, supply risk that investors should price into European AI-dependent business models.
insights.locator: Similarly, the growing scarcity of AI computing power

insights.claim: EuroStack, Europe's most prominent AI-sovereignty initiative, is both unrealistic and insufficiently ambitious for a near-term powerful-AI scenario; if it fails, Europe ends up fully dependent rather than achieving partial success.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner, Jakob Hensing
insights.so_what: The authors reject the leading European 'digital sovereignty' policy framework as the wrong bet for investors/policymakers tracking EU tech strategy.
insights.quote: its approach will not yield some partial success but a dangerously exposed and ultimately fully dependent Europe

insights.claim: Europe's best LLM developer, Mistral, ranks far behind both U.S. and Chinese frontier models, and even maximal European funding is unlikely to close that gap given that better-resourced players like Elon Musk and Mark Zuckerberg have failed to catch the frontier.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner, Jakob Hensing
insights.so_what: Undercuts the investment case for betting on European frontier-model champions catching up to U.S. leaders.
insights.quote: Europe’s best LLM, Mistral, currently ranks far behind the U.S. frontier models

insights.claim: Even EuroStack's own proponents concede Europe won't build frontier models, only ones 'a few steps behind' — an optimistic claim given European AI labs remain vulnerable to being cut off from U.S. hardware and compute infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner, Jakob Hensing
insights.so_what: Highlights the compute/hardware chokepoint as the binding constraint even for a scaled-down European AI ambition.
insights.quote: Europe is not going to build large frontier models but we can still build models a few steps behind which will be useful.

insights.claim: Europe controls only about 5% of the world's AI computing power and is falling further behind, as publicly led gigafactory investments face delays and meager EU funding while European industry favors less compute-hungry applied-AI approaches over large-scale data center investment.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner, Jakob Hensing
insights.so_what: A hard capacity constraint that caps any European frontier-AI ambition regardless of funding rhetoric — a key metric to track for EU AI policy credibility.
insights.quote: Europe currently only has 5 percent of the world’s computing power and is falling further behind.

insights.claim: The authors argue Europe should abandon 'AI superpower' ambitions and instead act as a middle power, building leverage together with other middle powers — Canada, India, Japan, South Korea, and the United Kingdom — rather than pursuing full independence from the U.S.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner, Jakob Hensing
insights.so_what: Defines the authors' recommended geopolitical positioning for EU AI policy — coalition-building over autarky.
insights.quote: Europe should assume its role as a middle power
insights.locator: Rather than banking on AI superpower delusions

insights.claim: Europe holds real chokepoint leverage via ASML's lithography machines, essential for high-performance chip manufacturing, and Siemens Energy's gas turbines, needed for rapid U.S. data-center expansion — but this leverage is blunted by Europe's near-total military dependence on the United States.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner, Jakob Hensing
insights.so_what: Identifies specific European firms/chokepoints (ASML, Siemens Energy) that carry outsized geopolitical leverage in the AI buildout — relevant for positioning around European industrials tied to the AI supply chain.
insights.quote: ASML’s lithography machines, without which no high-performance chips can be manufactured, and Siemens Energy gas turbines, without which rapid data center expansion in the United States is barely possible

insights.claim: Nearly $700 billion was invested in the current AI paradigm globally in 2026 alone, and if this bet pays off and Anthropic-style 'powerful AI' is achieved, second-best models would likely be inadequate to guard against critical cyber and security risks.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner, Jakob Hensing
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of the AI capex supercycle and frames model-tier access, not just any AI, as the security-relevant threshold.
insights.quote: with almost $700 billion invested alone in 2026
insights.locator: Any European AI strategy needs to account

insights.claim: The U.K. AI Security Institute's evaluations confirmed Anthropic's Mythos 5 capabilities were not merely hyperbolic marketing, validating that frontier-model access — not just strong AI regulation — is what protects against advanced AI risks.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner, Jakob Hensing
insights.so_what: Empirical confirmation from an independent evaluator that frontier capability gaps are real and security-relevant, reinforcing the case for Europe securing model access over regulation-only strategies.
insights.quote: the U.K. AI Security Institute’s evaluations did confirm that Anthropic’s highly guarded initial release was not just an elaborate charade
insights.locator: We received a taste of this scenario

insights.claim: Europe should invite U.S. hyperscalers facing rising domestic opposition to data centers to build that capacity on European soil instead, which reduces Europe's own investment risk and gives U.S. firms an incentive to lobby for European model access.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner, Jakob Hensing
insights.so_what: A concrete policy lever — European data-center siting for U.S. hyperscalers — that could be a growth driver for European real estate/energy tied to AI infrastructure.
insights.quote: Europe should invite U.S. hyperscalers and consortia facing growing public opposition at home to build data centers on the continent.

insights.claim: Fast permitting processes and rapid grid connections are the single most important factor for expanding European compute capacity, according to a Carnegie Endowment study — more important than the amount of public funding committed.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner, Jakob Hensing
insights.so_what: Identifies permitting/grid speed, not capital, as the binding bottleneck for European AI infrastructure buildout — a concrete policy metric to monitor.
insights.quote: the single-most important factor here is fast permitting processes and rapid grid connections
insights.locator: As a recent study by the Carnegie Endowment

insights.claim: Germany's Schwarz Group and a 75 billion euro SoftBank investment in France are cited as examples of the domestic and partner capital Europe needs to draw on for AI compute buildout, alongside U.S. investment.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner, Jakob Hensing
insights.so_what: Concrete capital flows to track as bellwethers of European AI infrastructure investment momentum.
insights.quote: the recent 75 billion euro SoftBank investment in France
insights.locator: Investments should be drawn from Europe itself

insights.claim: The EU has already amended its AI Act to regulate industrial AI systems differently from broad consumer AI, a change the authors say should be extended to the EU Data Act to support Europe's industrial-AI competitive advantage.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner, Jakob Hensing
insights.so_what: A concrete regulatory tailwind for European industrial-AI and robotics companies to watch for follow-through in the Data Act.
insights.quote: It is a positive step that the EU amended its AI Act to treat industrial AI systems differently

insights.claim: Capturing greater AI-enabled economic value will require large-scale mobilization of European capital via capital markets integration and pension system reform, alongside avoiding a selloff of proprietary European data assets and innovations to foreign buyers.
insights.speaker: Thorsten Benner, Jakob Hensing
insights.so_what: Flags EU capital-markets union and pension reform as structural preconditions for a credible European industrial-AI investment thesis.
insights.locator: To capitalize on its industrial base
